message.content: Adversaries may compromise third-party DNS servers to support their operations, leveraging DNS traffic for Command and Control (C2) and other malicious activities. By altering DNS records, they can redirect an organization's traffic, aiding in data collection and credential access. This tactic can also involve the use of Digital Certificates to mimic legitimate network communications, enhancing the stealth of their operations. Additionally, adversaries might create subdomains pointing to malicious servers without alerting the DNS server owner. The potential impact includes unauthorized data access, traffic redirection, and compromised network integrity. Mitigation strategies include monitoring DNS traffic for anomalies, implementing DNS security extensions (DNSSEC), regularly auditing DNS records, and employing network segmentation to limit the impact of compromised DNS infrastructure.
